summary: Spolsky warns retail investors against buying bonds from full-service brokers, particularly callable bonds that contain hidden embedded options. He presents a puzzle with three government bonds where Bond C appears to offer the highest interest rate but actually contains a call option that lets the government repay early if rates drop or keep your money if rates rise. This means the investor loses in both scenarios — stuck with low rates when rates rise, or forced to reinvest at lower rates when rates fall. The seemingly higher interest rate on callable bonds never truly compensates investors for the option they're implicitly selling. Spolsky argues that calculating the true value of these embedded options requires sophisticated tools most retail investors don't have, which is exactly how Wall Street brokers profit from the arrangement.
key_points: Callable bonds contain embedded options that benefit the issuer at the investor's expense: if rates drop, they repay early; if rates rise, they keep your money locked in at the low rate
The higher stated interest rate on callable bonds is designed to disguise the value of the option the investor is implicitly selling to the issuer
Calculating the true value of embedded bond options requires yield curves, implied volatility data, and sophisticated tools like Bloomberg terminals that retail investors don't have
Full-service brokers systematically exploit this information asymmetry, designing callable bonds specifically to appear attractive while extracting hidden value from retail investors
In the example, Bond C appeared to pay the highest rate but was actually worth $2,000 less than the straightforward alternatives
money_quotes: Never, ever, ever buy bonds at retail from a full-service broker. Especially municipal bonds.
The typical commissions they take will tend to lop off a year of interest, so the day you buy the bond, you've lost money.
And these people buy bond C, too. And they are getting ripped off.
You're screwed either way. The only way you're not screwed is if rates stay approximately the same.
When you buy Bond C, you're giving up something valuable. You're giving the government an option to call your bond.
The retail brokers selling these bonds have snuck a sneaky derivative into the bond, and you can't calculate what it's worth unless you do this for a living.

Most bonds sold to investors at retail by full service brokers are disguised to look like they pay a lot of interest when the truth is that the high interest rates never really compensate you for the option you're implicitly selling.
Full service brokers, in this day and age of low cost mutual funds and discount brokers, are really nothing more than machines for ripping off retail investors.
key_insight: Callable bonds are designed to exploit information asymmetry between Wall Street and retail investors, using higher stated interest rates to disguise the valuable option the investor is unknowingly selling to the issuer.
